changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "What's a Drug That Makes You Commit Suicide?"

TextENGNew: ``Let me get straight to the point... is there a drug that can make someone kill themselves?``
``Not directly, no.``
``That’s a roundabout answer. ...So you’re saying there’s an indirect way?``
``What I mean is that it’s possible to induce a mental state in which someone becomes suicidal.``
``...That’s getting complicated. What exactly is a “suicidal mental state”?``
``Well, from a general psychological standpoint, it’s said that suicide attempts most often occur during the transition from an introverted state to an extroverted one.``
Bipolar disorder is distinct from clinical depression.
Depression produces only a deeply negative mental state,@kbut bipolar disorder alternates between that negative, depressive state and an extremely active manic state.
changeReason: Old says manic state "accompanies" the depressive state; new correctly says they "alternate" (交互に), reflecting that bipolar disorder cycles between states rather than having them simultaneously.

TextENGNew: ``During the introverted phase, the individual loses all confidence and becomes deeply pessimistic. But they don’t commit suicide.@k@vS20/00/443200024. They lack even the willpower to take their own life.@k@vS20/00/443200025. ...And during the opposite phase, they don’t commit suicide either.``
changeReason: Old omits the third line 逆の状態もまた自殺をせん ("the opposite state also doesn't lead to suicide"), and has the missing closing quotation mark fixed in new.

TextENGNew: ``In that state, they’re overflowing with confidence and energy—they feel like everything is going swimmingly. So naturally, they have no reason to end their life.``
changeReason: Old includes material from the previous entry ("The opposite state doesn't lead to suicide, either") that doesn't belong in this line; also "walking on air" changed to "going swimmingly" which better matches 順風満帆 (smooth sailing).

TextENGNew: ``...I see. So neither state leads to suicide on its own, but when the transition between them occurs, that’s when it happens?``

``During the introverted phase, the desire to die exists, but they lack the energy to carry out such a monumental act. However, as the extroverted phase begins, their motivation gradually returns and they regain control of their body.``
changeReason: "Gain a burst of willpower" changed to "motivation gradually returns" -- Japanese says 徐々に (gradually), contradicting the sudden "burst" in the old translation.

TextENGNew: ``I see! So in other words, they recover the energy needed to go through with suicide.``
``Precisely.@k@vS20/00/443200028a. That’s why medication is prescribed during that transitional period—to keep them from acting on those impulses.``
``...So was Tomitake in this kind of mental state?``
``Generally speaking, suicide is carried out through deliberate, culturally understood methods. Tools are involved.@k@vS20/00/443200030. It’s completely different from self-mutilation!``
changeReason: Old paraphrases 道具を使用して (using tools) as "They prepare"; new correctly translates it as "Tools are involved."

TextENGNew: ``Tomitake’s death was hardly what you’d call deliberate...``
changeReason: "Civilized" changed to "deliberate" -- 文化的 means cultural/civilized (matching the forensic investigator's previous terminology about 文化的な手法), not "deliberate" which has a different meaning.

TextENGNew: ``...Then perhaps it’s most natural to assume some kind of intoxication was involved. Please tell me about that drug you mentioned—the one that induces a suicidal mental state.``
changeReason: Old asks about a "drug that causes someone to commit suicide" but the Japanese specifies 自殺したくなる精神状態を起こす薬 (a drug that induces a mental state of wanting to commit suicide), and 中毒 (intoxication) is rendered as "foreign substance" in old but "intoxication" in new.

TextENGNew: ``There are reports that certain analgesics can cause an excessive psychological swing from introverted to extroverted.``
changeReason: "Violent" changed to "excessive" -- 過剰 means excessive/extreme, not violent.

TextENGNew: ``...Additionally, abnormal consumption of drugs with hypnotic properties has been reported to cause erratic behavior, though it’s not very common.``
``Neither of those showed up in the autopsy report. ...Are there any other possibilities?``

``The only remaining possibility would be illness.@k@vS20/00/443200034. Diseases that cause thyroid abnormalities have been reported to produce similar symptoms. But those all come with distinctive symptoms of their own.@k@vS20/00/443200035. The deceased exhibited none of them.``
``Isn’t there something more sudden? Something that fits this case—an acute condition that would make someone want to kill themselves on the spot.``
``Let me think... well, there’s the possibility of psychological disturbance caused by brain damage.``
``This can be caused by drug intoxication, but also by physical trauma to the brain or certain diseases.``
changeReason: Old says "physical trauma" generally; new specifies "physical trauma to the brain" matching the Japanese 脳の外傷 (brain trauma).

TextENGNew: ``So you’re saying it’s possible to fall into an abnormal mental state even without drugs.``

``The deceased was surrounded by his attackers with his life in danger, right?@k@vS20/00/443200039. Prolonged extreme stress, compounded by hormonal irregularities, and on top of that, an unlucky blow to the head causing brain damage that triggered self-destructive behavior... it’s a possibility, at least.``
changeReason: Old adds "after being chased" which is not in the Japanese (犯人に囲まれて = surrounded by criminals); also "messed with his brain chemistry" oversimplifies 分泌異常 (hormonal irregularities) and the new more accurately translates the chain of causation.

TextENGNew: ``...Could you perhaps put that in simpler terms...?``
``Ha ha ha! Put simply—he bonked his head during the brawl, and that made him go haywire.``

``Bwah ha ha ha ha ha ha!!! So the attackers didn’t actually intend to kill him, then.@k@vS20/11/440700031. They just wanted to rough him up for some pocket change and happened to hit him in the wrong spot!``
Two portly middle-aged men shared a vulgar laugh.
changeReason: Old omits 中年 (middle-aged) and mistranslates 下品に (vulgarly) as "hoarsely"; new correctly describes them as "portly middle-aged men" with a "vulgar laugh."

TextENGNew: ``...But of course, that’s not what happened.``
``Ahem. ...Indeed.``
``Whether it was habitual drug use or something psychological, the deceased’s identity holds the key. How’s that side of the investigation going?``
changeReason: "Background" changed to "identity" -- 身元 specifically means identity/personal details, not background.

TextENGNew: ``Oh my, look at the time! I’d better head back before Kuma-chan gets upset with me.``
``Right! Keep at it!@k@vS20/00/443200044. And have a good one!``
``Same to you!``
